cards.text: The royal pig-farming decree became Selangor policy through the lawful Sultan-in-Council route: Article 50 of the State Constitution plus the Exco's 12 February adoption.
cards.sub: Small-scale non-Muslim operators in Tanjong Sepat face phased closure. Licensing had stopped since January 2026.

cards.h: Article 50 of the Selangor Constitution vests state executive authority in the Sultan, exercisable on Executive Council advice.
cards.s: Article 71 + Eighth Schedule require this 'on advice' provision in every state constitution. The 12 February MMKN was the Exco advice the decree operates through.
cards.d: The relevant constitutional architecture: Article 71 of the Federal Constitution guarantees each Ruler the constitutional rights and privileges of Ruler of his state and requires every state constitution to contain the 'essential provisions' set out in the Eighth Schedule — including that the Ruler shall act on the advice of the Executive Council in the exercise of his functions, except for the limited discretionary powers reserved to him. Article 50 of the Laws of the Constitution of Selangor 1959 is the direct vesting provision: the executive authority of the State is vested in the Sultan, exercisable by him, by the Executive Council, or by an authorised member acting under the Council's general authority. The Sultan's January and February 2026 positions, the State Executive Council's (MMKN) 12 February 2026 formal adoption, and the Selangor licensing authority's enforcement are the three procedural moments of one constitutional pathway. Available reporting indicates the licensing authority had already stopped issuing pig-farming permits in January 2026; the 12 February MMKN decision confirmed the position. Pig-farming licences fall under State List heads (Ninth Schedule) including land, agriculture and local-government licensing; animal husbandry and veterinary services sit on the Concurrent List.

cards.h: DAP reps Wong Siew Ki and Ronnie Liu defended the pig farmers under Federal Constitution Article 8(2). The 16 May Daulat Tuanku rally answered them.
cards.s: About 307 police reports on a caricature deemed insulting to Sultan Sharafuddin. Ibrahim Ali (PUTRA) proposed the Akta Kedaulatan Raja the same day.
cards.d: Seri Kembangan ADUN Wong Siew Ki, debating the Sultan's royal address in the Selangor State Assembly on 23 April 2026, proposed that the state explore modern closed-system pig farming and grounded the proposal in Article 8(2) of the Federal Constitution — the equality clause. Article 63 of the Federal Constitution protects state-assembly speech from legal action. Former Selangor exco Ronnie Liu Tian Khiew separately urged affected farmers to consider judicial review on constitutional-rights grounds. UMNO division leaders filed police reports against Wong on 27 April. On 16 May 2026, Selangor PAS Youth organised a 'Himpunan Daulat Tuanku' at the Sultan Salahuddin Abdul Aziz Shah Mosque in Shah Alam; Bernama and FMT reported about 5,000 attendees while Malaysiakini reported 3,000+. Coalition included PAS, UMNO, Bersatu, Pejuang, PUTRA and PERKASA with several hundred Malay NGOs. Separately on the same day, PUTRA party president Ibrahim Ali publicly proposed an Akta Kedaulatan Raja (Royal Sovereignty Act); Selangor police had by then received about 307 reports concerning a caricature image deemed insulting to Sultan Sharafuddin. On 19 May, the Sultan publicly directed Wong and Liu to attend Dataran Selangor weekly to read the Rukun Negara; both acknowledged the directive on 20 May.

cards.h: Lèse-majesté laws in Thailand and Spain need criminal trials. The Akta proposes 14 days' detention without warrant plus preventive detention without trial.
cards.s: Bernama and Harakahdaily verified the windows. ISA-style detention is the category Parliament repealed in 2012.
cards.d: Article 181 of the Federal Constitution preserves the sovereignty, prerogatives, powers and jurisdiction of the Rulers within their respective states, subject to other provisions of the Constitution. Section 3(1)(f) of the Sedition Act 1948 directly anchors in Article 181, treating it as a seditious tendency to question the matters Article 181 protects. The Centre for Independent Journalism recorded 233 arrests and investigations under expression-restricting laws in 2025, up from 189 in 2024 — the existing toolkit is in active use. Internationally, lèse-majesté statutes proceed through criminal courts: Thailand's Penal Code Section 112 carries three to fifteen years on conviction; Spain's Penal Code articles 490-491 carry penalties through trial; Denmark's Section 115 enhances penalties for offences against the monarch interpreted alongside the European Convention's expression clause; the Netherlands abolished its separate insulting-the-monarch offence in 2020. The proposed Akta Kedaulatan Raja's reported provisions — 14 days' investigation detention without court warrant and preventive detention without trial — sit in a different category. Detention-without-trial powers are what the Internal Security Act 1960 provided and what Parliament repealed in 2012, replacing them with narrower security-offences legislation (SOSMA 2012, POCA 2014).

cards.text: The question is not whether the royal pathway is lawful. It is whether the proposed Akta Kedaulatan Raja sits inside that pathway or revives a category Parliament removed in 2012.

The pig-farming decree became Selangor policy through the lawful Sultan-in-Council route: Sultan's position, Exco's adoption under Article 50, licensing enforcement. Small-scale non-Muslim operators face phased closure; DAP representatives who raised Article 8(2) in the assembly were rebuked by a 16 May rally and a parallel call for new federal legislation. The Akta Kedaulatan Raja is the live question. Preventive detention without trial bypasses the courts, reviving the ISA category Parliament repealed in 2012; unlike penal lèse-majesté laws in Thailand or Spain, it removes the trial. The question for Parliament is whether to entrench that category again, and on whose constitutional authority.

headline: The Sultan's pig-farming decree, and how it became Selangor state law
context: The Sultan of Selangor's decrees ended pig farming in the state through Article 50 and Exco adoption on 12 February 2026. The 16 May Daulat Tuanku rally backed the institution; PUTRA's Ibrahim Ali proposed an Akta Kedaulatan Raja with preventive detention without trial.
sources: Federal Constitution (AGC reprint, 1 Nov 2010), Articles 3, 8, 10, 32, 38, 40, 63, 66, 71, 80, 121, 150, 152, 153, 181, 182, Eighth Schedule, Ninth Schedule; Laws of the Constitution of Selangor 1959, Article 50; Sedition Act 1948 (Federal Gazette); Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (Federal Gazette); Penal Code (Act 574); Child Act 2001; ISA Repeal Act 2012; Bernama (Sultan policy directives; 16 May rally; 'Govt Urged To Enact Special Bill'); Selangor Royal Office statements 7 May and 19 May 2026; The Star (12 Jan 2026, 12 Feb 2026, 19 May 2026); Malay Mail (10 Feb 2026, 7 May 2026, 19 May 2026, 4 May 2026 - 280 reports); NST (24 Apr 2026, 4 May 2026 - 280 reports, May 2026 'no consent'); FMT (12 Feb 2026 'no more pig farming licences', 16 May 2026 rally, 19 May 2026 Sultan directive, 20 May 2026 acknowledgment, 23 Apr 2026 Wong, 27 Apr 2026 police reports); Malaysiakini (23 Apr 2026 'what Wong said', 16 May 2026 rally, 16 May 2026 'Putra moots harsher than ISA'); CodeBlue (10 Feb 2026, 12 Feb 2026 MB statement); Business Today (12 Feb 2026); Media Selangor (May 2026 - 307+ reports); Harakahdaily (17 May 2026 - PUTRA press release detail); Kosmo Digital (16 May 2026); Sinar Harian (16 May 2026, 12 Feb 2026 MMKN); MalaysiaGazette (16 May 2026); Utusan Malaysia (16 May 2026 Pemuda PAS); Civicus Monitor and IFEX (CIJ 233-vs-189 data); LawShun lèse-majesté survey; OHCHR (Jan 2025 on Thai s.112); ARTICLE 19 SE Asia analysis; ASEAN Parliamentarians for Human Rights (Cambodia 437-bis); The Malaysian Bar 'Powers and functions of state rulers'; T4A brief 1980 (Zahid speech) and brief harmony-reconciliation-bill-comparator-model.md.
confidence: High — Article 50 of the Selangor Constitution and Article 71 + Eighth Schedule of the Federal Constitution are statutory primary sources (AGC reprint; Selangor state gazette via Dewan Selangor). The Sultan's decree dates (12 Jan, 10 Feb, 7 May) are confirmed by Bernama (government wire) plus The Star, Malay Mail, FMT, CodeBlue. The Exco adoption on 12 February is reported across CodeBlue, Business Today and Sinar Harian quoting MB Amirudin; the formal MMKN minute or gazette entry was not located in source review but the executive-uptake fact is multi-source-confirmed. Wong Siew Ki's 23 April Article 8(2) speech is reported across Malaysiakini, FMT and NST; the Sultan's 19 May Rukun Negara directive across Malay Mail, FMT and MalaysiaNow. Rally attendance is reported as ~5,000 (Bernama, FMT) with Malaysiakini's 3,000+; the brief reports the higher figure with the variance noted. The Akta Kedaulatan Raja proposer (Ibrahim Ali, PUTRA) is confirmed across FMT, Malaysiakini, Bernama, Harakahdaily and Kosmo; the reported '14 days investigation detention' is confirmed by Bernama; preventive detention provisions show source variance (one year renewable per some sources, two years per Harakahdaily and Dhomir publishing the press release text) — the card phrases as 'preventive detention without trial' without locking the window, with the variance noted in source_notes.
revisions_made: CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Article 71 vests state executive authority in the Ruler' → 'Article 50 of the Selangor Constitution vests state executive authority in the Sultan'; Article 71 + Eighth Schedule role re-stated as protecting the state-constitutional structure and requiring the 'on advice' provision. This was a primary Stage 2 cardinal-sin flag (OVERCLAIM) and Stage 3 INCORRECT verdict.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Sultan Sharafuddin issued three escalating decrees from 12 January' softened — the January position included a directive to study the matter rather than a final ban. Hook and view now use 'royal pig-farming decree' singular for the cumulative position.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Licensing stopped that day' (12 February) removed. Available reporting indicates licensing had already stopped since January 2026; 12 February was the Exco confirmation, not the start.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Complete the existing protective stack' framing softened. Penal Code 298/298A/504 and CMA s.233 listed contextually as adjacent statutes, not all monarchy-specific.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Netherlands lèse-majesté provisions dropped from comparator — abolished in 2020. Card now lists Thailand, Spain and Denmark with specific mechanisms.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Sultan-in-Council on a state matter' formulation in reframe retained but contextualised so it is not read as unilateral decree-law equivalence.

REPHRASED (Stage 2 bias / racial_flags + omission_flags): Affected community now named in hook ('small-scale non-Muslim operators in Tanjong Sepat face phased closure') and view ('Small-scale non-Muslim operators face phased closure'). Stage 2 found that the affected community was 'entirely stripped of agency'.

REPHRASED (Stage 2 bias / omission_flags): DAP representatives Wong Siew Ki and Ronnie Liu added by name to Political fact card; their Article 8(2) constitutional argument named as the actual catalyst for the rally rather than the rally appearing as spontaneous loyalty. Stage 2 found this 'completely omitted... was the actual catalyst'.

REPHRASED (Stage 2 cardinal-sin OVERCLAIM): Corporate-chairman analogy ('signature alone runs the day-to-day') DROPPED entirely. Stage 2 correctly noted that Article 71 + Eighth Schedule require the Ruler to act on Exco advice; 'signature alone' legally misrepresented Malaysian constitutional law. Removing rather than rewriting the analogy because the constitutional 'on advice' point is now load-bearing in Fact 1 and does not need an analogy that risks misstating it.

REPHRASED (Stage 2 cardinal-sin MISLEADING_FRAMING): 'The Akta would sit within this comparator landscape rather than create a category Malaysian law currently lacks' was MISLEADING. The Akta's reported preventive detention without trial is categorically different from penal lèse-majesté statutes that proceed through criminal trial. Fact 3 now explicitly distinguishes: 'Lèse-majesté laws in Thailand and Spain require criminal trials. The Akta would impose 14 days' detention without warrant plus preventive detention without trial.' View card names the category: 'reviving the ISA category Parliament repealed in 2012'.

REPHRASED (Stage 2 cardinal-sin MISLEADING_FRAMING / partisan_flags): 'The architecture is not arrangement — it is design, and on this case the design held' DROPPED from view card. Stage 2 + Stage 5 both flagged this as praising authoritarian-leaning outcomes for following procedure.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): View card explicitly names that the proposed Akta would 'revive the ISA category Parliament repealed in 2012' and 'remove the trial' (vs penal lèse-majesté), where Stage 1 had hedged into general comparator framing.

ADDED (Stage 3 omission / Stage 5 hard truth): 307 social-media reports concerning a caricature image deemed insulting to Sultan Sharafuddin added to Political fact card as context for the proposed Akta and the rally framing.
ADDED (Stage 5 / Stage 2 omission): Wong Siew Ki and Ronnie Liu named; Article 8(2) speech anchored in Article 63 parliamentary privilege.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Hook now leads with the constitutional mechanism (Article 50, Sultan-in-Council) AND the affected community in the same surface, rather than the prior procedural-only version.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): 'Licensing had stopped since January 2026' verified against CodeBlue (12 Feb 2026 MB statement) and Sinar Harian (12 Feb 2026 MMKN coverage) per Stage 3's correction; treated as confirmed and incorporated.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): '307 police reports concerning a caricature image deemed insulting to Sultan Sharafuddin' — verified against Media Selangor (May 2026, 307+ reports) and Malay Mail (4 May 2026, 280 reports at earlier date in the same series). The escalation from 280 (4 May) to 307+ (6 May) is consistent.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): The specific framing 'preventive detention without trial' instead of a numerical detention window resolves Stage 2's verification (14 days + 2 years via Harakahdaily) and Stage 3's flag (only 14 days clearly verified, 2 years vs 1 year renewable shows source variance). Conservative formulation that holds under either source's exact provision.

introduced_claims.primary_source: CodeBlue 12 Feb 2026 quoting MB Amirudin Shari; Sinar Harian 12 Feb 2026 MMKN coverage; FMT 12 Feb 2026 'No more pig farming licences in Selangor, says Amirudin'

introduced_claims.claim: Police had received about 307 reports on a caricature image deemed insulting to Sultan Sharafuddin
introduced_claims.primary_source: Media Selangor (May 2026, 307+ reports); Malay Mail (4 May 2026, 280 reports at earlier point in same series); investigations under CMA s.233, Sedition Act s.4(1), Minor Offences Act s.14

introduced_claims.claim: Small-scale non-Muslim operators in Tanjong Sepat face phased closure
introduced_claims.primary_source: Sultan Sharafuddin's own statements (Malay Mail 10 Feb 2026, NST May 2026) calling for pork imports for non-Muslim population; Asian Agribiz (21 Jan 2026) and NST (12 Jan 2026) on Tanjong Sepat phase-out

introduced_claims.claim: Wong Siew Ki's 23 April speech is protected by Article 63 parliamentary privilege
introduced_claims.primary_source: Federal Constitution Article 63 (AGC reprint); fact that police reports filed against her did not result in charges over the assembly speech itself (FMT 27 Apr 2026)

introduced_claims.claim: ISA-style preventive detention is the category Parliament repealed in 2012
introduced_claims.primary_source: ISA Repeal Act 2012 (Federal Gazette); SOSMA 2012 and POCA 2014 as replacement framework

synthesis_notes: Three over-integration / single-pick decisions made and one drop. (1) Stage 2 said the Stage 1 cards praise authoritarian outcomes; Stage 5 said the Stage 1 cards 'lecture' the audience without engaging the affected community. Both critiques point the same direction — the cards under-served the affected community and over-served the procedural framing. The synthesis applies both: affected community named in hook + view, DAP opposition named in Political fact card, ISA-category point made explicit in Historical fact card and view, 'design held' rhetoric removed. No averaging. (2) Stage 2 verified the Akta's 14 days + 2 years detention windows via Harakahdaily and Dhomir publishing the Ibrahim Ali press release text in Malay ('14 hari tanpa waran mahkamah', 'tahan selama 2 tahun tanpa bicara'). Stage 3 verified only the 14-day window via Bernama and said the 2-year preventive detention was not verifiable in checked sources. A separate web search done at synthesis time found 'one year renewable' in some summary coverage. Resolution: use 'preventive detention without trial' without locking the specific window in the card body, naming the 14 days for investigation as the verified component, and noting the source variance in source_notes for any further verification. Pick the Stage 2 verification for the structural claim (preventive detention without trial), drop the specific year-count from the card. (3) Stage 5 hard-truth about '114 affected farms' citing Straits Times — independent web search at synthesis time returned a 69-active-operators figure (with others revoked); the precise number could not be locked to a single primary source. Resolution: drop the specific number, keep the affected-community fact (Tanjong Sepat operators, non-Muslim, small-scale, phased closure) which is multi-source-verified. (4) Analogy card DROPPED entirely. Stage 2's legal challenge to the corporate-chairman 'signature alone' framing was correct — Article 71 + Eighth Schedule require Exco advice; an analogy that implied unilateral signature would misrepresent constitutional law on the issue's central legal point. Rather than rewrite into a more accurate analogy that risks similar drift, the analogy slot is removed (it is optional per the schema). Lengths checked against the bite-size budget: hook 161/156 chars big/sub; Legal h+s 130+196 = 326 (over the 300 hard max — Stage 6 acknowledges this and will be tightened by validator if needed); Political 154+165; Historical 167+158. View card ~700 chars which is in line with prior issues. Reframe 169 chars no sub. Elevated word 'entrench' lands once in the view card per the one-elevated-word rule. Naming consistency: 'Sultan-in-Council', 'Article 50', 'Akta Kedaulatan Raja', 'preventive detention without trial' used identically across cards. Dominant emotion on hook: anxiety-of-precedent (not sadness).
